Advances in the metabolic engineering of Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Yarrowia lipolytica for the production of β-carotene

Qi Guoa School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, People’s Republic of China;b College of Biotechnology and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, People’s Republic of China
,
Qian-Qian Penga School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, People’s Republic of China
,
Ya-Wen Lia School of Food Science and Pharmaceutical Engineering, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing, People’s Republic of China
,

,

Abstract

β-Carotene is one kind of the most important carotenoids. The major functions of β-carotene include the antioxidant and anti-cardiovascular properties, which make it a growing market. Recently, the use of metabolic engineering to construct microbial cell factories to synthesize β-carotene has become the latest model for its industrial production. Among these cell factories, yeasts including Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Yarrowia lipolytica have attracted the most attention because of the: security, mature genetic manipulation tools, high flux toward carotenoids using the native mevalonate pathway and robustness for large-scale fermentation. In this review, the latest strategies for β-carotene biosynthesis, including protein engineering, promoters engineering and morphological engineering are summarized in detail. Finally, perspectives for future engineering approaches are proposed to improve β-carotene production.

Graphical abstract

Keywords:

  • β-Carotene
  • Saccharomyces cerevisiae
  • Yarrowia lipolytica
  • metabolic engineering
  • microbial cell factories

Introduction

Carotenoids represent the largest class of natural pigments found in nature. More than 750 types of carotenoids were identified in: bacteria, fungi, algae and higher plants. Based on their chemical structure, carotenoids can be classified into the carotenes such as β-carotene and the xanthophylls such as lutein. Carotenoids have antioxidant properties that have led to a range of important medicinal and industrial applications [Citation1]. β-carotene is one kind of the most important carotenoids. β-carotene is a tetraterpenoid (C40H56) containing eight isoprene units with a highly unsaturated chain that forms a chromophore responsible for its yellow-orange color [Citation2,Citation3]. β-carotene was first isolated in 1831 by Wachenroder, who crystallized the pigment from the typically orange roots of carrots (Daucus carota) and named the compound accordingly [Citation4]. The major benefits for β-carotene include its role as a precursor of vitamin A, which is involved in multiple processes such as (i) maintenance of the chromophores in the eye, (ii) prevention of blindness and night-blindness, (iii) ensuring reproductive efficiency, and (iv) maintaining healthy growth. Furthermore, the contribution of β-carotene to the color of foods such as margarine, soft drinks, and baked goods ranges from yellow to orange depending on the concentration [Citation5–7]. According to the Global β-carotene Market: Industry Analysis and Forecast (2019–2026), β-carotene had a market value of US $442.1 million in 2018, which is anticipated to reach more than US $700 million by 2026, corresponding to an annual growth rate of 5.94% [Citation8].

Currently, the primary sources of β-carotene include chemical synthesis, plant extraction, and microbial fermentation. Among these, chemical synthesis mainly uses: acetone, butadiene, di-aldehyde, and methanol as substrates, which result in cumbersome synthesis steps and low yields. Plant extraction is usually limited by high cost, seasonal variation, and low productivity. By contrast, microbial fermentation is a promising strategy for sustainable production to meet market demands because of its economic competitiveness while safeguarding the environment [Citation9,Citation10]. In addition to plants and algae, some microorganisms can also synthesize β-carotene, including fungi and bacteria. Among fungi, filamentous fungi such as Blakeslea trispora as well as yeasts such as Phaffia rhodozyma and Torulopsis candida are used to produce β-carotene through fermentation. Among bacteria, Erwinia uredovora and Pantoea agglomerans are the main producers [Citation11]. These wild strains have the advantages of a short growth cycle, fast metabolism, and higher efficiency than plants and algae, but their disadvantages of unstable fermentation and low yield have made it difficult to achieve industrialization.

In recent years, metabolic engineering and synthetic biology have contributed remarkably to the use of microbes as biological factories [Citation12]. Metabolic engineering has enabled heterologous carotenoid biosynthesis in industrially important non-carotenoid-producing strains, such as: Escherichia coli [Citation13–15], Yarrowia lipolytica, and Saccharomyces cerevisiae. Microbial sources of carotenoids have received increasing attention because of the restricted rules and regulations currently applied to obtaining chemicals. The most important step toward heterologous production is the selection of an appropriate host species in which to engineer the pathway. When selecting a host species for a heterologous pathway, properties such as ease of cloning, ease of culturing, and suitability of the host for the new enzymes and compounds are considered [Citation16]. Yeasts are among the most frequently used chassis strains in synthetic biology and many species have well developed techniques for cloning, culturing, and industrial scale-up, resulting in excellent performance as cell factories for various valuable biochemicals [Citation17–20]. Among the available yeast strains, S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica have attracted most attention as generally regarded as safe (GRAS) organisms with mature genetic manipulation tools [Citation21]. They possess a native mevalonate pathway with high throughput for heterologous synthesis of isoprenoids, including β-carotene. Furthermore, their robustness to the cultivation environment lays a good foundation for large-scale fermentation. In addition, Y. lipolytica, which is considered one of the most potential β-carotene-producing strains, also exhibits other unique advantages. As an oleaginous yeast, Y. lipolytica can accumulate large amounts of lipid to store highly hydrophobic terpene molecules including β-carotene in intracellular oil droplets [Citation18]. Apart from this, Y. lipolytica could utilize a variety of carbon sources, such as: glycerol, fatty acids, and alkanes with the active tricarboxylic acid cycle and a pentose phosphate pathway. It is worth mentioning that the highest reported β-carotene yield was obtained in Y. lipolytica, with titers of 39.5 g/L [Citation20]. In general, these two yeasts were used as hosts for the heterologous production of some important carotene-derived compounds such as vitamin A [Citation22,Citation23], astaxanthin [Citation24–26] and β-ionone [Citation27–32].

In this review, we focus on progress within the last ten years in the metabolic engineering of yeasts as microbial cell factories for the biosynthesis of β-carotene. Specifically, the β-carotene biosynthetic pathways and the different engineering strategies for β-carotene biosynthesis are discussed in detail. This review also focuses on perspectives for further improving β-carotene production in yeasts by analyzing the achievements, challenges and cutting-edge technologies of metabolic engineering and synthetic biology.

Biosynthetic pathway of β-carotene

The β-carotene biosynthesis pathway mainly includes two relatively independent modules, respectively dedicated upstream precursor production and downstream β-carotene synthesis. As shown in , in the upstream precursor production module, the universal precursors isopentenyl diphosphate (IPP) and dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMAPP) be synthesized through two different endogenous or exogenous pathways.

Figure 1. β-Carotene synthesis pathways in yeast. Abbreviations: ERG10; acetyl-CoA thiolase; ERG13; HMGCoA synthase; tHMG1; truncated 3-hydroxy-3-methyl glutaryl coenzyme A reductase; ERG12: mevalonate kinase; ERG8: phosphomevalonate kinase; ERG19: mevalonate diphosphate decarboxylase; IDI: IPP isomerase; ERG20: geranyl/farnesyl diphosphate synthase; GGS1: geranylgeranyl diphosphate synthase; CarRP: bifunctional phytoene synthase/lycopene cyclase; CarB: phytoene dehydrogenase; CCD1: carotenoid cleavage dioxygenase 1; crtW: β-carotene ketolase; crtZ: β-carotene hydroxylase; LiuC: enoyl-CoA hydratase; AibAB: glutaconyl-CoA decarboxylase; cbjALD: acyl-CoA reductase; YahK: aldehyde reductase; ThiM: hydroxyethylthiazole kinase from E. coli; IPKMTH: isopentenyl phosphate kinase from M. thermautotrophicus; ScCK: choline kinase from S. cerevisiae; AtIPK: isopentenyl phosphate kinase from A. thaliana; IUP: isopentenol utilization pathway; IPA: isoprenoid alcohol pathway; MVA: mevalonic acid pathway.

The natural endogenous pathways include the mevalonic acid (MVA) pathway and the 2-C-methyl-d-erythritol-4-phosphate (MEP) pathway. Yeasts use the MVA pathway which starts with the condensation of two acetyl-CoA molecules to acetoacetyl-CoA, which is subsequently converted into 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl coenzyme A (HMG-CoA). Mevalonate is generated by the action of the rate-limiting enzyme HMG-CoA reductase (HMGR), after which consecutive phosphorylations and decarboxylation of mevalonate result in IPP and DMAPP.

The isoprenoid alcohol (IPA) pathway and the isopentenol utilization pathway (IUP) are two alternative exogenous pathways that can also be introduced to produce IPP and DMAPP. The IPA pathway is centered on isoprenoid alcohol and can be divided into an isoprenoid alcohol generation module and an isoprenoid alcohol utilization module. Hydroxyethyl thiazole kinase (ThiM) from E. coli and isopentenyl phosphate kinase (IPKMTH) from Methanothermobacter thermautotrophicus were expressed to convert isoprenoid alcohol into IPP and DMAPP. In contrast with the prokaryote-derived IPA pathway, the IUP pathway uses eukaryotic choline kinase from S. cerevisiae (ScCK) and isopentenyl phosphate kinase from Arabidopsis thaliana (AtIPK).

In the downstream β-carotene synthesis module, the basic five-carbon (C5) units IPP and DMAPP are ligated in diverse proportions to produce different pyrophosphate precursors, such as the C10 geranyl pyrophosphate (GPP), C15 farnesyl pyrophosphate (FPP), and C20 geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate (GGPP). Finally, co-expression of the three exogenous genes: GGPP synthase (crtE), phytoene dehydrogenase (carB) and bifunctional phytoene synthase/lycopene cyclase (carRP) convert two GGPP molecules into orange-colored β-carotene through: condensation, dehydrogenation and two cyclization reactions. Therefore, efficiently redirecting FPP flux toward the β-carotene synthesis pathway is crucial [Citation18]. GGS1 and multifunctional carotene synthases from different species have been heterologously expressed to enhance β-carotene production, including the enzymes form: Xanthophyllomyces dendrorhous [Citation7,Citation33], Mucor circinelloides [Citation20,Citation34], Schizochytrium sp. [Citation35] and Blakeslea trispora [Citation36]. In addition, some related carotenoids can also be synthesized by using β-carotene as a precursor. For example, β-ionone can be obtained by introducing carotenoid cleavage dioxygenase 1 (CCD1) into β-carotene-producing strains [Citation27,Citation29]. Astaxanthin can be obtained by introducing β-carotene ketolases (crtW) and hydrolyases (crtZ).

Strategies for enhancing β-carotene accumulation in yeast

With the rapid development of synthetic biology, β-carotene production in microbial cell factories has become an active field of research. Many efforts have been devoted to increasing the efficiency, stability, and cost-effectiveness of β-carotene biosynthesis. Strategies such as morphological engineering, organelle engineering, and protein engineering have achieved remarkable results. Recent advances in the metabolic engineering of these organisms for β-carotene biosynthesis are summarized in . For better understanding, is provided to summarize different strategies.

Figure 2. Overview of metabolic engineering strategies for improving β-carotene production in yeast. (I) Increasing the supply of acetyl-CoA. (II) Increasing the mevalonate pathway flux. (III) Redirecting FPP flux. (IV) Cofactor engineering. (V) Introducing a heterologous pathway. (Ⅵ) Modulating lipid metabolism. (VII) Morphological Engineering. (Ⅷ) Organelle engineering. (Ⅸ) Protein engineering. (Ⅹ) NHEJ-mediated chromosomal integration. (Ⅺ) Promoter engineering. (Ⅻ) Adaptive laboratory evolution.

Table 1. Production of β-carotene in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Yarrowia lipolytica.

Download CSVDisplay Table

Enhancing the precursor supply of acetyl-CoA

Precursor availability is a key limiting factor in the metabolic engineering of microorganisms. Acetyl-CoA is a key node of central metabolism and numerous researchers are focusing on rewiring acetyl-CoA metabolism for the biosynthesis of a wide range of industrially relevant chemicals. β-carotene biosynthesis in engineered yeast is usually derived from the mevalonate (MVA) pathway with cytosolic acetyl-CoA as a precursor, which results in a bottleneck in the supply of acetyl-CoA to the MVA pathway.

Commonly employed strategies for enhancing the supply of acetyl-CoA include the remodeling of endogenous pathways and the introduction of exogenous acetyl-CoA regeneration pathways. Five alternative cytosolic acetyl-CoA pathways were engineered in Y. lipolytica, including the: pyruvate-acetate route (encoded by pyruvate decarboxylase pdc, aldehyde dehydrogenase aldH, and the native acetyl-CoA synthase acs), the pyruvate-aldehyde route (encoded by pyruvate decarboxylase pdc and CoA-acetylating aldehyde dehydrogenase aad), pyruvate formate lyase (encoded by pflAB), acetyl-CoA shuttling pathway (encoded by carnitine acetyltransferase cat2), and the non-oxidative pentose-phosphate pathway (encoded by phosphoketolase xfp and phosphotransacetylase pta). Notably, researchers compared the influence of different pathways on both the lipid titer and oil content, and the results indicate that the alternative cytoplasmic acetyl-CoA pathway does indeed play a key role in lipid biosynthesis [Citation59]. This strategy was also successfully applied to produce carotene-derived downstream products. Moreover, the PK-PTA pathway was introduced in Y. lipolytica to improve β-ionone production. Different combinations including two heterologous PKs and two heterologous PTAs were compared. The final strain showed a 32% higher β-ionone titer, which reached 202.2 ± 13.6 mg/L [Citation29].

In addition to increasing the flux of acetyl-CoA, the disruption of competing pathways to reduce the consumption of branch precursors is also an effective strategy. The glyoxylate cycle consumes acetyl-CoA via cytosolic malate synthase (Mls1) and peroxisomal citrate synthase (Cit2). Studies have shown that the deletion of Mls1 and/or Cit2 can prevent acetyl-CoA consumption and improve product biosynthesis. In a study by Huang et al. the removal of the Cit2 gene caused a two-thirds increase in the acetyl-CoA concentration and improved squalene production [Citation60]. In general, increasing the flux of acetyl-CoA is an effective strategy to improve terpenoid production, which also provides a reference for the synthesis of β-carotene.

Increasing the mevalonate pathway flux

A sufficient flux through the mevalonate pathway is crucial for the accumulation of carotenoids. Overexpression of the key rate-limiting enzymes is the most commonly used approach among the published strategies because it can directly divert the precursor from the mevalonate pathway to β-carotene synthesis. HMG-CoA reductase is generally considered to be the first rate-limiting enzyme in the mevalonate pathway. In numerous studies additional HMGR genes or truncated HMG1 variants (tHMG) were overexpressed to increase carotenoid production [Citation12,Citation24,Citation34,Citation40,Citation53,Citation61].

In addition to HMG-CoA reductase, geranylgeranyl diphosphate synthase (GGS1) is another rate-limiting enzyme, which is involved in the condensation of farnesyl pyrophosphate (FPP) to geranylgeranyl pyrophosphate (GGPP). If GGS1 activity is insufficient, the condensation of two FPP molecules is limited, which results in increased synthesis of the by-product squalene and reduces the metabolic flux toward β-carotene production. Zhang et al. overexpressed GGS1 in Y. lipolytica, increasing the β-carotene titer from 17.1 ± 0.5 to 40.4 ± 0.2 mg/L [Citation34]. Recently, Ma et al. exploited the branching point at the FPP node to create a metabolic flow restrictor and regulate the flux toward lycopene to maintain its concentration at sub-inhibitory levels, while allowing high lycopene conversion into β-carotene. After screening five different GGPPS, the lycopene levels were reduced and β-carotene production increased to 1.26 g/L with 92.5% selectivity using GGPPS from Sulfolobus acidocaldarius [Citation20]. This is an effective strategy to increase β-carotene production by removing lycopene substrate inhibition.

In addition to these two key rate-limiting enzymes, additional enzymes in the mevalonate pathway are also considered to promote β-carotene production [Citation27,Citation53]. For example, Qiang et al. expressed two copies of ERG13, which resulted in a 259% increase of the product titer [Citation57]. Gao et al. overexpressed the entire MVA pathway, increasing β-carotene production by 46.9% compared to the strain without enhancement of MVA synthesis [Citation53]. These results suggested that increasing the mevalonate pool, including overexpressing a single rate-limiting enzyme or the entire mevalonate pathway, is an effective strategy for increasing β-carotene production.

Redirecting FPP flux toward the β-carotene synthesis pathway

Deletion or downregulation of competing pathways in the host is a common strategy to increase precursor titers. FPP is the direct precursor for β-carotene production, but is primarily used for squalene production in the native pathway.

Squalene is the precursor for the synthesis of sterols, which are necessary for cell membrane formation. As a result, the strategy of directly knocking out squalene synthase (ERG9) is not feasible since it would lead to cell death. In order to increase the availability of FPP for carotenoid biosynthesis, the most commonly used strategy is to downregulate the flux toward squalene. Many strategies such as the protein destabilization method [Citation62] and promoter engineering have been proven very successful in increasing terpene production in yeast. Truncating the endogenous squalene promoter or replacing it with inducible promoters or weak promoters to downregulate the mRNA expression of squalene synthase are the most commonly used strategies. In Y. lipolytica, the glucose-repressible PALK1 and PICL1 promoters, as well as the ergosterol promoter PERG1 have been used to downregulate squalene biosynthesis [Citation53]. In addition, Kildegaard et al. truncated the native promoter of the squalene synthase gene (SQS1) to 500, 100 or 50 base pairs or replaced it with weak promoters, resulting in 2- to 2.5-fold increases of the β-carotene titer compared to the reference strain with the native SQS1 promoter. The shortening of the promoter to 50 bp resulted in the highest β-carotene titer of 797.1 ± 57.2 mg/L [Citation24]. Similarly: the repressible MET3 promoter [Citation63], the glucose sensing HXT1 promoter [Citation64], ergosterol-responsive promoters [Citation65], and the copper-repressible CTR3 promoter [Citation66] have been applied to downregulate squalene synthesis in S. cerevisiae. Recently, Bu et al. replaced the native ERG9 promoter with an OA-repressible promoter (PIZH1) to dynamically downregulate ERG9 expression, which diverted the metabolic flux toward the β-carotene pathway, resulting in a 31.7% increase of the β-carotene titer without adversely affecting cell growth [Citation39].

Furthermore, the addition of ergosterol biosynthesis inhibitors is also an effective strategy to limit the accumulation of squalene and ergosterol, driving more FPP into the carotenoid biosynthetic pathway. Recently, Yan et al. showed that adding 100 mg/L ketoconazole alone can result in a 15.6% increase of β-carotene concentration in S. cerevisiae. Furthermore, the combination of overexpressing the HMG-CoA reductase gene and adding ketoconazole resulted in a 206.8% increase of pigment content [Citation61]. All these studies indicate that downregulation of squalene synthesis can effectively improve β-carotene production. At the same time, the combination of different strategies will have a better effect.

Cofactor engineering

Cofactors are involved in enzymatic reactions and regulate the chemical equilibrium state. As a consequence, cofactor levels determine the metabolic flux. In yeast cells, NADPH is the main form of reducing power for biosynthesis. However, the overexpression of the MVA pathway genes, such as: tHMG1, HMG2, and ERG9 can deplete cytosolic NADPH. Accordingly, the expression of NADPH regeneration-related genes has become a popular approach to stimulate the synthesis of terpenes and other reduced metabolites. A number of different enzymes have been successfully used to increase the supply of NADPH, including: glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (ZWF1), NADP-specific isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDP2), 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase (GND2), mannitol dehydrogenase (MnDH1, MnDH2), succinate semialdehyde dehydrogenase (UGA2), specific glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH3), and malic enzyme (MAE) [Citation67]. For example, ZWF1 and POS5 were individually overexpressed in a carotenoid-producing strain of S. cerevisiae, resulting in 59.9 and 81.4% increases of the lycopene and β-carotene titers, reaching 2.50 ± 0.11 and 0.53 ± 0.03 mg/L, respectively [Citation68]. Increasing the supply of NADPH was shown to enhance the production of: carotenoids [Citation68], xylitol [Citation69], erythritol [Citation70], terpenoids [Citation71] and squalene [Citation72]. However, this strategy does not always work well. The influence was more pronounced in low-producing strains than in high-producing strains, which may be because these cofactors are involved in multiple modules rather than a single step of the biosynthesis pathway.

Introducing an artificial isopentenol pathway to enhance precursor flux

The MVA pathway is the most important pathway of isoprenoid production in yeast, which requires cofactors such as NADPH and ATP, as well as competing with central carbon metabolism. In recent years, a new synthetic pathway for the biosynthesis of isoprenoids using the substrate isopentenol, dubbed the isopentenol utilization pathway (IUP), was demonstrated to be a promising alternative to the native mevalonate (MVA) pathway. The IUP pathway requires only three enzymatic steps (CK, IPK, and IDI) and ATP as a sole cofactor for the synthesis of IPP and DMAPP, which has shown significant potential because of its simplicity and decoupling from central carbon metabolism. In recent studies, the IUP pathway has been successfully used for the production of: isoprenoids [Citation73], amorphadiene [Citation74], α-farnesene [Citation75], and lycopene [Citation76]. Introduction of the IUP pathway in Y. lipolytica increased the β-carotene titer by 23%, to 4.22 g/L [Citation20].

The isoprenoid alcohol (IPA) pathway is another alternative synthetic pathway that centers around the synthesis and subsequent phosphorylation of IPA. Compared with the IUP pathway, only two enzymes (CK and IPK) in the lower IPA pathway are different. Clomburg et al. demonstrated that the synthetic isoprenoid pathway could use a single unrelated carbon source to produce different isoprenoid compounds, including: geraniol, limonene, farnesol, diaponeurosporene, and lycopene [Citation77]. Given its modularity and design directly linked to central carbon metabolism, expression of the IPA pathway could be explored in yeast to further unlock the potential for β-carotene synthesis. However, introducing the entire IPA pathway is complex compared to the IUP pathway. Accordingly, further studies are needed to achieve intracellular adaptation for β-carotene production such as the regulation of cofactors and ATP.

Modulating lipid metabolism to improve β-carotene storage

The limitation of storage space is one of the major challenges for efficiently overproducing carotenoids in engineered microorganisms. The lipophilic nature of carotenoids promotes their storage in the lipid bodies of the cells, so that improving lipid metabolism could increase β-carotene production. Most studies to date concentrated on two strategies.

The most commonly used strategy is to increase the intracellular lipid content for β-carotene storage. Ma et al. overexpressed three key genes associated with fatty acid synthesis [endogenous phosphatidate phosphatase (PAH1), diacylglycerol acyltransferase (DGA1), and a mutated acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC1) variant], which increased TAG accumulation and lycopene production. Subsequently, they overexpressed a fatty acid desaturase (OLE1) and deleted the seipin gene (FLD1), which regulates the size of lipid droplets [Citation78]. The resulting S. cerevisiae strain displayed increased TAG production of 74.9 mg/g DCW and increased lycopene production of 61.7 mg/g DCW. Similarly, Larroude et al. found that the lipid-overproducing strain of Y. lipolytica is better suited for the production of β-carotene than a wild-type background. They concluded that the contents of carotene and lipids are positively correlated [Citation12]. Luo et al. overexpressed ACC1 and DGA1 in Y. lipolytica and also supplemented palmitic acid to improve lipid accumulation, both of which promoted carotenoid synthesis [Citation76].

In view of the above results, adding hydrophobic substrates is also a promising strategy. Hydrophobic substrates including oleic acid (OA), waste oil and glycerin can be hydrolyzed to produce either fatty acids or glycerol for reassembly as lipids. For example, Sun et al. showed that the addition of oleic acid and palmitoleic acid significantly increased β-carotene production [Citation49]. Bu et al. added 2 mM OA, which significantly improved the triacylglycerol metabolism of lipid bodies and resulted in a 36.4% increase of the β-carotene content [Citation39]. In general, increasing TAG production significantly promoted β-carotene accumulation.

Morphological engineering

Dimorphic growth is common in many species of ascomycete yeasts, including S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica. The plasticity of the yeast, pseudohyphal and hyphal morphological form allows yeasts to adapt and invade new environments in response to external conditions. Carbon, nitrogen sources, temperature, osmotic pressure, osmolarity and pH of the medium have been found to affect yeast morphology [Citation79]. This trait is essential for fungi in natural environments, but also represents a challenge for their industrial utilization, as maximum economic efficiency demands that bioreactors be run at high temperature and osmolarity using low-quality nutrients, all of which may initiate the switch to hyphal growth. In addition, strong overexpression of heterologous pathways and production of target compounds often results in metabolic stress that impairs cell growth, which is detrimental for the production of target metabolites. The Kss1/Cek1 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway [Citation80] and the cAMP-protein kinase A (PKA) pathway [Citation81,Citation82] are two major signal transduction pathways that control the dimorphic switch. Recently, Liu et al. deleted the CLA4 and MHY1 genes to convert the mycelium back to the yeast form, which further relieved the metabolic stress and increased β-carotene production to 7.6 g/L [Citation56].

Organelle engineering

While most engineered metabolic pathways are located in the yeast cytoplasm, there is growing interest in subcellular compartmentalization which has demonstrated advantages for the production and accumulation of desirable compounds in several cases because of the potential to eliminate metabolic crosstalk and to enhance the efficiency of compartmentalized pathways [Citation83]. In yeast, acetyl-CoA is widely present in the mitochondria, peroxisomes, and cytoplasm, where it serves as a universal precursor for the production of terpenoids and carotenoids. The feasibility of harnessing different organelles such as peroxisomes [Citation84], and mitochondria [Citation85] for the production of terpenoids has been extensively investigated. Recently, Ma et al. targeted the astaxanthin pathway to all three compartments including lipid bodies, the endoplasmic reticulum and peroxisomes by enzyme fusions strategy, which achieved a total astaxanthin titer of 858 mg/L in flask fed-batch fermentation and it was the highest reported so far in yeast [Citation86]. Based on the advantages of organelle engineering, target organelles must be carefully selected for metabolic pathways with different characteristics, so as to make full use of their potential.

Protein engineering

Site-directed mutagenesis is an indispensable molecular tool for the directed evolution of enzymes, opening avenues for the development of tailor-made enzymes. Site-directed mutagenesis can improve enzyme stability and substrate specificity, which has been an indispensable tool in protein engineering to enhance functional properties. Recently, this technique has also been used to remove lycopene substrate inhibition and enable high carotenoid productivity [Citation20]. A CarRP variant (Y27R) with a complete loss of substrate inhibition was obtained without reduction of enzymatic activity. By expressing this variant, a β-carotene titer of 39.5 g/L was achieved (98% selectivity), representing the highest value to date. Furthermore, the authors utilized the CarRP variant (E78K) combined with a highly active GGPPxd derived from X. dendrorhous to construct a dedicated lycopene-producing strain. These findings illustrate that site-directed mutagenesis can have profound effects on pathway fluxes, providing a new paradigm for controlling metabolism.

The design of chimeric and fusion enzymes has been a successful strategy in metabolic engineering. The fusion of two enzymes modulates their spatial organization, which is probably the simplest useful strategy for modifying pathway fluxes. For example, the expression of a three-enzyme fusion protein encompassing CrtB, CrtI, and CrtY led to a 2-fold increase of β-carotene production [Citation87].

NHEJ-mediated chromosomal integration

Integration of genes into the chromosome is a more stable approach for heterologous gene expression. Canonically, organisms employ either homologous recombination (HR) or non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) to repair DNA double-strand breaks (DSBs). NHEJ-mediated integration can insert DNA fragments into different genomic locations, which often results in different expression levels.

NHEJ can not only be used to construct multigene metabolic pathways in a short time, but also enables the integration of multiple gene copies [Citation88]. NHEJ-mediated random integration was used in strain construction and enzyme activity screening to optimize the metabolic pathway, as well as to construct a random expression library. The NHEJ strategy has been used to improve the biosynthesis of various terpenoids [Citation89]. In a recent study, Cui et al. constructed a LIP2 lipase expression library and a library of multiple genes in the β-carotene biosynthesis pathway [Citation52]. Liu et al. used NHEJ-mediated integration to construct a genome-scale mutagenesis library, which allowed the rapid identification of new targets that improved β-carotene biosynthesis and acetic acid tolerance [Citation54]. Recently, they also conducted related research on the NHEJ strategy. They constructed an NHEJ-mediated GFP expression library to identify potential neutral integration sites with high gene expression levels and integration efficiency. Subsequently, the lycopene biosynthesis pathway was integrated into the identified neutral integration sites with different combinations of promoters and terminators. Finally, they obtained different levels of lycopene-producing strains [Citation90]. These studies showed the potential of the NHEJ strategy for screening high-production strains.

Promoter engineering

Promoters are especially important for controlling gene expression. When driving recombinant gene expression to optimize metabolic pathways, promoter strength is usually the most important parameter to be considered. Commonly used promoters can be divided into two main classes. Constitutive promoters offer stable expression levels across varying culture conditions, while dynamic or inducible promoters enable dramatic changes of expression levels in response to environmental stimuli. Strong constitutive promoters are often used for engineering applications. Liu et al. designed and screened the base motifs and tested the activities of yeast artificial core promoters, offering an approach to redesign promoters with artificial sequences and improve β-carotene production [Citation51]. Li et al. designed a de novo mutant UAS library with a broad dynamic range, and built a prediction model based on DNA curvature and transcription strength to characterize the library. Finally, they applied the synthetic UAS library to regulate β-carotene synthesis [Citation37]. However, the constitutive expression of certain proteins and metabolic pathways can be detrimental to cell growth due to product toxicity. In such scenarios, it is desirable to utilize dynamically regulated promoters to realize the dynamic control of gene expression. The galactose regulatory network in S. cerevisiae is one of the most well-characterized transcriptional induction systems. Xie et al. employed the GAL regulatory system to control the time of β-carotene synthesis [Citation45]. In the GAL regulatory system, Mig1p functions as a transcriptional repressor to repress the transcription of GAL4 which was only controlled by the Snf1 signaling pathway when GAL80 was deleted. Gal4p can bind to the GAL10 and GAL1 promoters to activate the transcription of downstream genes and the pathway switch time could be controlled by adjusting the glucose supply. Finally, the engineered strain produced 11 mg/g DCW of total carotenoids (72.57 mg/L) and 7.41 mg/g DCW of β-carotene in shake-flask culture, demonstrating the effectiveness of this regulatory strategy.

Adaptive laboratory evolution

Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) is a powerful tool that exploits the metabolic plasticity of strains to adapt to challenging environmental conditions and to improve desired product yields [Citation91]. Studies have shown that ALE enables the accumulation of beneficial mutations that can further optimize the metabolic machinery in a non-intuitive way. ALE relies on forced selection of specific phenotypes after accumulation of spontaneous mutations over many generations under constant selection pressure, which results in improved microbial strains. Based on the ALE strategy, yeasts were successfully engineered for increased tolerance to toxic substances, thereby increasing the product yield [Citation92,Citation93]. ALE was also successfully applied to improve the production of β-carotene. For example, taking into account the antioxidant properties of carotene, Godara et al. used hydrogen peroxide as the selective pressure to enhance the β-carotene yield of S. cerevisiae. The result showed that the best β-carotene production mutants exhibited increased lipid and β-carotene biosynthesis [Citation94]. Although ALE shows great potential, a lot of work is required to identify the underlying causal mutations. It is therefore desirable to develop a powerful approach for rapid trackable mutagenesis.

Transcriptional factor regulation

The evolution of complex cellular phenotypes requires the simultaneous tuning of the expression of many genes, which can be achieved by reprograming global transcriptional regulators. Li et al. [Citation48], as well as Yang et al. [Citation55] integrated the ESCRT protein complex subunit (DID2) into S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica, respectively. The results revealed that the DID2 gene could promote transcription elongation and the protein level of key enzymes in the β-carotene synthesis pathway. Furthermore, DID2 gene increased the mRNA level of HXK in the glycolytic pathway, thereby promoting glucose utilization. In addition, DID2 gene also increased the mRNA level of ZWF and TKL in the pentose phosphate pathway and SucAB in the tricarboxylic acid cycle, thereby promoting cofactors (ATP and NADPH) consumption. Overall, DID2 gene plays an important role in β-carotene biosynthesis and also benefits the production of many other natural products. The central Spt15 factor, as a TATA-binding protein, is able to globally control the transcription of various metabolic and regulatory genes, whose mutations could result in pleiotropic changes of gene transcription levels. Wang et al. designed a global transcription engineering strategy in Y. lipolytica. They constructed combinatorial mutagenesis of SPT15 and screened the optimal mutant combinations for enhanced β-carotene production [Citation95].

Fermentation

Substrate utilization

The carbon source is considered a crucial factor for the production of carotenoids. In most cases, glucose is used as the substrate for cell growth and product formation. However: other sugars, glycerol, lipids, acetate, and lignocellulosic biomass have been considered as potential alternatives due to their relative low cost, additional sustainability benefits and better conversion into products in some cases.

Lignocellulosic biomass

In the past decade, metabolic engineering and synthetic biology have contributed remarkably to the use of microbes as cell factories by broadening the use of lignocellulose [Citation96,Citation97]. Xylose, which can be derived from nonedible sources, is the second most abundant sugar in nature, comprising up to 30-40% of lignocellulosic biomass [Citation98]. Although S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica are unable to catabolize xylose efficiently, the development metabolic engineering has facilitated the application of yeasts as a platform for bioconversion of xylose into various value-added products [Citation99]. Two pathways including the xylose reductase (XR) and xylulose dehydrogenase (XDH) pathway (the XR-XDH pathway), and the xylose isomerase (XI) pathway (the XI pathway) were heterologously expressed in yeasts to convert xylose into xylulose [Citation100–102]. Sun et al. observed increased production of β-carotene from xylose compared to glucose in engineered S. cerevisiae SR8B [Citation42]. It is possible that xylose avoids the production of ethanol associated with the Crabtree effect, which promotes an adequate supply of cytosolic acetyl-CoA and enhances the production of acetyl-CoA-derived products. Furthermore, Cheng et al. utilized xylose-enriched hydrolysate generated from bioenergy sorghum to produce β-carotene in engineered S. cerevisiae SR8B. The final β-carotene titer reached 114.50 mg/L using 66 g/L high-concentrated hydrolysate xylose sirup [Citation47]. All these reports suggest that xylose utilization is a promising strategy for overproduction of β-carotene and other isoprenoids in yeast.

Lipid

Adding lipids as substrates is also an effective strategy to increase the β-carotene yield, with many advantages. On one hand, some lipids accumulate inside the cells, providing a lipophilic environment for carotenoid accumulation. On the other hand, a part of the lipids would be metabolized in the cells via the β-oxidation pathway, generating abundant acetyl-CoA as a precursor for β-carotene. Most importantly, some hydrophobic substrates, such as oil-containing wastes, have almost zero cost and their utilization can reduce the burden on the environment. In view of these advantages, many studies have been conducted to improve β-carotene production. For example, Worland et al. employed biomass growth analyses and 13C isotope tracing to investigate lipid feedstock utilization by Y. lipolytica. The study showed that canola oil-containing yeast-peptone (YP) resulted in the best β-carotene titer (121 ± 13 mg/L), 2-fold higher than the titer obtained in glucose-based YP medium [Citation58]. Moreover, Sun et al. also showed that supplementation with 60 mg/L oleic and palmitoleic acid respectively led to 83.7 and 130.2% increases of β-carotene content in recombinant S. cerevisiae, in parallel with recovery of the unsaturated fatty acid content in the cells [Citation49]. Fathi et al. used triolein or olive oil as the sole carbon source in engineered S. cerevisiae [Citation33]. All these studies demonstrated the potential of lipid supplementation for improving β-carotene production.

Optimization of fermentation conditions

Used for microbial production of β-carotene and other carotenoids, factors such as light, temperature, pH and dissolved oxygen can also greatly influence the product titer [Citation8]. Compared with yeasts such as S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica, which were extensively genetically engineered, studies of some well-known carotenoid-producing yeasts such as Rhodotorula glutinis and R. mucilaginosa have generally focused more on culture conditions. However, there are also studies devoted to improving the β-carotene production through culture conditions in the engineering yeasts. For example, Shi et al. found that while the recombinant strain grew faster at 30 °C, it accumulated much more β-carotene at 20 °C, which may be related to the transcriptional levels of genes at different temperatures [Citation103]. Lv et al. developed a DO-stat culture strategy to investigate the changes in ATP levels, NADP+/NADPH ratio and gene expression levels during fed-batch fermentation. Finally, 94 g/L biomass and 2.01 g/L β-carotene were obtained, which were about 28% higher than that in non-optimized fed-batch fermentation [Citation9]. Furthermore, Luo et al. developed a two-stage pH control strategy in which the pH was maintained at 7.0 for the first 24 h and then shifted to 5.0 after 24 h. The strategy improved the β-carotene production by 19.1% compared to the maximum values obtained in constant pH fermentation [Citation104]. In general, it is necessary to explore and optimize fermentation conditions for both engineered and wild-type strains to improve β-carotene production.

Conclusions and perspectives

The application of β-carotene as a colorant and antioxidant has increased the interest of industry and academia to develop a low-cost process for β-carotene production. Yeasts such as S. cerevisiae and Y. lipolytica offer advantages of easy genetic manipulation, a thorough understanding of metabolism, fast growth, and existing fermentation technologies for large-scale production. With the advancement of genetic and metabolic engineering approaches, the construction of high-yield carotenoid production strains as well as the application of these techniques for large-scale carotenoid production have become feasible. Great progress has been made in increasing β-carotene production by optimizing metabolic pathways combined with expanding the scope of substrate utilization.

This review aimed to show an overview of the biotechnological processes responsible for β-carotene production, and illustrate the possibility to manipulate these processes to achieve low costs, high yields and short processing times. Although significant achievements have been made in the production of β-carotene using the yeast platform, there is still room for improvement. For example, enzymes in heterologous pathways are often limited in activity and specificity, and site-directed mutagenesis is a promising approach to improve the substrate preference and specificity of enzymes, which is receiving increasing attention. This strategy is expected to further improve β-carotene biosynthesis. However, these efforts rely on readily available protein crystal structures, so efficient structure-driven design should be the focus of future efforts, which depend heavily on the quality of computational modeling and the simulation accuracy of substrate docking at their binding sites. In addition, according to the characteristics of carotene, a specific high-throughput detection method should be established for inexpensive screening of large mutant libraries, which can quickly and efficiently obtain potential high-yielding strains. Last but not least, the increasing complexity of novel pathways introduced into cells makes precise regulation of metabolic fluxes necessary. Building global network models combined with a series of data and computing technologies, such as: genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, etc., will be able to achieve balanced regulation and make large-scale biotechnological β-carotene production economically feasible.

Author contributions

GQ, STQ and YC provided the idea. GQ, LYW, STQ, PQQ and YC wrote the manuscript. STQ, LYW, YF and WYT revised the manuscript.

Disclosure statement

No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

Additional information

Funding

                                                                                                                                                                                                                    Author(作者)
                                                                                                                                                                                                                    Tian Zhen: A medical student majored in dermatology with great interest in programming.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                    田振：一名对编程感兴趣的皮肤科医学生。欢迎同我交朋友。
